romania.summary: Relația dintre (pre)adolescenți, părinți și școală pare, astăzi, mai fragilă ca oricând. Între dorința copiilor de autonomie, nevoia părinților de control și presiunea unui sistem educațional care încearcă să țină pasul cu schimbările sociale, apar tensiuni, neînțelegeri și, uneori, rupturi greu de reparat. Andreia Bodea, directoarea Colegiului Național „Ion Luca Caragiale” din București, vorbește într-un …

Printre fanii noii identităţii vizuale a „câinilor” se numără Marian Ştefan, directorul general al Poştei Române, a cărui postare elogioasă a adunat zeci de like-uri, inimioare şi haha-uri în social media. „Nu eram dinamovist până acum, dar […] The post Directorul Poştei Române, despre noua siglă a lui Dinamo: E bestială! appeared first on Times New Roman .

romania.summary: Cine suntem noi și de cine avem nevoie? Suntem echipa de la Telefonul Vârstnicului, serviciu social licențiat, singura linie telefonică gratuită și confidențială, accesibilă la nivel național, dedicată persoanelor vârstnice, ce oferă consiliere socială și alinarea singurătății. În fiecare an suntem sunați de peste 5,000... Post-ul Angajăm Asistent Social pentru Telefonul Vârstnicului la București! apare prima dată în Fundația Regală Margareta a României .

romania.summary: Într-o casă modestă din județul Vaslui, unde mirosul de lemne arse se amestecă cu zgomotul pașilor mici de copii și... Articolul „Școala e drumul vostru”. Învățăturile unei mame cu 10 clase. apare prima dată în World Vision România .

romania.title: De Paște, 900 de vârstnici singuri primesc prezență umană, ascultare și speranță
romania.summary: În timp ce pentru mulți români Paștele este sărbătoarea revederii și a meselor în familie, pentru mulți vârstnici singuri aceste zile sunt printre cele mai grele din an. Singurătatea doare, lipsurile și sentimentul de abandon se simt mai apăsat tocmai atunci când toți ceilalți par... Post-ul De Paște, 900 de vârstnici singuri primesc prezență umană, ascultare și speranță apare prima dată în Fundația Regală Margareta a României .

romania.title: Sprijinul Raiffeisen Bank schimbă vieți. Povestea lui Victor din programul „Învață să reușești”
romania.summary: United Way România lucrează acolo unde nevoia este cea mai mare, acolo unde sprijinul nu este doar util, ci vital. Fără acest ajutor, mulți copii ar rămâne în urmă, riscând să se piardă în dificultățile vieții. De aceea, apreciem sprijinul pe termen lung pe care Raiffeisen Bank îl oferă programului „Învață să reușești”. Printre copiii […] Articolul Sprijinul Raiffeisen Bank schimbă vieți. Povestea lui Victor din programul „Învață să reușești” apare prima dată în United Way Romania .

romania.title: Bullyingul în adolescență: răni invizibile și cum pot fi vindecate
romania.summary: De ce vorbim despre bullying Bullyingul nu mai este de mult o problemă „minoră” sau o etapă inevitabilă a copilăriei.... Articolul Bullyingul în adolescență: răni invizibile și cum pot fi vindecate apare prima dată în World Vision România .

medical.title: Need to see a psychologist? How to find one that's right for you
medical.summary: After weeks (or months) of putting it off, you book a longer appointment with your GP to talk about your mental health. You explain that you've been feeling low, anxious and overwhelmed. They suggest seeing a psychologist. But how do you know if this psychologist is the right person for you?

medical.title: Blood test reveals nine tumor cell 'neighborhoods' tied to immunotherapy outcomes
medical.summary: A simple blood test can reveal the geographic relationships among healthy cells surrounding a cancerous tumor, researchers at Stanford Medicine and the Mayo Clinic have found. The test is the first noninvasive way to study what's called the tumor microenvironment, which plays a critical role in determining how different patients—even those with similar tumors—fare after diagnosis and treatment.

medical.title: Testosterone suppresses brain tumor growth in males, study suggests
medical.summary: In a new study, scientists at Cleveland Clinic discovered that hormones associated with male development may play a key role in limiting the growth of brain tumors in men. The research team found that the loss of androgen hormones, such as testosterone, in a preclinical model of glioblastoma drove tumor growth by inducing local inflammation and triggering the production of stress hormones.

medical.title: 1,000 more steps: A simple, powerful prescription for surgical recovery
medical.summary: Researchers analyzing data from the National Institutes of Health's All of Us Research Program have found that a patient's step count following surgery is a powerful, objective predictor of recovery, outperforming other metrics such as heart rate variability and self-reported wellness. The study is published in the Journal of the American College of Surgeons.

medical.title: Scientists find blood-based biomarkers for inflammatory breast cancer
medical.summary: Researchers at The University of Texas MD Anderson Cancer Center and The University of Texas at Austin have identified specific blood-based genomic biomarkers that distinguish inflammatory breast cancer from other subtypes, providing a new and less invasive method for early diagnosis, disease progression monitoring and treatment development for patients with this aggressive disease.

medical.title: Concerns raised on gaps in health care for released prisoners
medical.summary: People leaving prison in England can experience avoidable gaps in their medication because of fragmented health care systems, poor information sharing, and discharge processes which are sometimes rushed due to release procedures, a new study has revealed.

medical.title: Why surgery still looks like an old boys' club
medical.summary: While entry into medicine and surgery has become more diverse, why does that diversity disappear at senior levels? A new study from the University of Surrey argues that the answer lies in how careers are judged day-to-day.

medical.title: Lying down to stand stronger: 10 minutes of simple floor exercises a day could improve balance and agility
medical.summary: Don't feel like standing up for a workout? No problem, you can still get a good workout that helps improve static standing balance, flexibility, and agility, all while lying on your back with your head facing up.

medical.title: Baltimore emergency rooms see spike in asthma-related cases after night heat waves
medical.summary: Hospitals in Baltimore see an uptick in asthma-related emergency room cases in the weeks following nighttime heat waves in their neighboring communities, according to new research from Johns Hopkins University.

medical.title: At-home blood test and brain testing could screen people for dementia risk
medical.summary: A finger prick blood test combined with online brain testing—all done from home—could one day effectively identify people's risk of developing dementia, according to a new study.

science.title: Diaspora distress: When geopolitical conflict follows immigrant workers into the office
science.summary: Rostam does not sleep through the night anymore. At 2 a.m., when his phone buzzes, he's awake before the sound finishes. It might be his parents calling from Tehran, on a connection that is unreliable, sporadic and sometimes cut off mid-sentence. He has learned not to miss those calls, because the next one may not come for days.

science.title: Webb and Hubble find massive star clusters emerge faster
science.summary: Astronomers using the NASA/ESA/CSA James Webb Space Telescope together with the NASA/ESA Hubble Space Telescope have looked deeply at thousands of young star clusters in four nearby galaxies, studying clusters at different stages of evolution. Their findings show that more massive star clusters emerge more quickly from the clouds they are born in, clearing away gas and filling the galaxy with ultraviolet light. The result gives us a better understanding of star formation in galaxies, as well as how and where planets can form.

science.title: What can singing mice say about human speech?
science.summary: Speech is a crowning achievement of human evolution, the skill that separates us from every other animal. So, it would stand to reason that evolving this capability required some enormous leap in brain complexity. A study published in Nature suggests otherwise.

science.title: How to build cities for wildlife, not just people
science.summary: In central Seoul, South Korea, a motorway once covered a buried urban stream. Today, that same stretch has been uncovered—a process known as daylighting—and this river is home to plants, fish and insects. This flowing water cools the city in summer and attracts tens of thousands of people every day. What used to be concrete now boosts biodiversity, the local economy and community well-being.

science.title: Hunters' appreciation of a targeted deer-management program transcends harvest
science.summary: Too many white-tailed deer are damaging forests in the U.S. by eating young plants before they can grow, limiting forest regeneration and damaging biodiversity. To mitigate this challenge, the Pennsylvania Game Commission implemented an initiative called the Deer Management Assistance Program (DMAP) that helps landowners manage deer populations by allowing hunters to harvest more female deer where they are overabundant. While hunters largely value and enjoy hunting in DMAP areas, they have some sentiments that may help inform wildlife management, according to a new study by Penn State researchers.

science.title: The lost koala: New fossil species was hiding in plain sight for 100 years
science.summary: In 2024, the Western Australian Museum received a donation. It was a koala skull collected from Moondyne Cave in Margaret River by Lindsay Hatcher, an avid caver. There was something a bit odd about this skull, and we were able to put our finger in it.

science.title: What working‑class boys need to succeed at school: Respect and open conversations
science.summary: Across the UK, working-class boys are navigating an unprecedented convergence of pressures. There are entrenched gaps between working-class boys and their peers in their levels of attainment at every stage of education.

science.title: Landsat 9 captures Russia's restless Shiveluch volcano mid-eruption
science.summary: Near-constant activity continues on the volcano in Russia. Shivelyuch (also called Shiveluch), the most northerly active volcano on the Kamchatka Peninsula, is one of the most active volcanoes in the world. On a near-daily basis, satellites detect new signs of activity within its horseshoe-shaped caldera, including thermal anomalies, hot avalanches and debris flows, and ash deposits that darken the surrounding landscape.

science.title: NASA’s Roman Poised to Transform Hunt for Elusive Neutron Stars
science.summary: Astronomers have long known that neutron stars, the crushed cores left behind after massive stars explode, should be scattered throughout the Milky Way galaxy. However, most of them are effectively invisible. A new study published in Astronomy and Astrophysics suggests NASA’s upcoming Nancy Grace Roman Space Telescope could spot them anyway. Using detailed simulations of […]

science.title: New NASA Technology Mimics Extreme Cold of the Lunar Night
science.summary: As NASA looks to explore the Moon, Mars, and beyond, researchers must develop materials capable of withstanding the extreme temperatures found in space and on other planets and their moons. In frigid conditions, rubber can shatter like glass, circuit boards may fail, and electrical connections can freeze and fracture.  Gaining a deeper understanding of how […]

science.title: Feeling underqualified can help drive performance or toxic behavior—depending on one psychological factor
science.summary: We've all been there: staring at a job description or a daunting new project and feeling a cold prickle of dread. You have the degree, maybe even the title, but looking at the task ahead, you feel like a total fraud. You're "underqualified"—and it feels like only a matter of time before everyone else figures it out.

science.title: Hologram technology where 'light becomes the key' enables hard-to-copy security
science.summary: A new type of hologram technology has been developed that uses the motion of light as a key, revealing information only under specific conditions. This is gaining attention as a novel approach that can simultaneously overcome the limitations of existing optical communication and security technologies.

science.title: Freshwater mussel protein offers new source of inspiration for medical-grade glues
science.summary: Researchers at the University of Toronto have identified a protein from the quagga mussel that can stick to surfaces underwater, even though it lacks a chemical feature long thought to be essential for this kind of adhesion. The protein, called Dbfp7, is the first freshwater mussel adhesive protein to be functionally characterized.

environment.title: This 2-pound dinosaur is rewriting what scientists know about evolution
environment.summary: A nearly complete dinosaur skeleton discovered in Patagonia is helping scientists crack the mystery of alvarezsaurs, a bizarre group of bird-like dinosaurs. The fossil of Alnashetri cerropoliciensis reveals that these animals became tiny before developing their later specialized features, such as stubby arms and ant-eating adaptations. Weighing under two pounds, the dinosaur is one of the smallest known from South America.

environment.title: Scientists finally solve the mystery of yeast’s tiny centromeres
environment.summary: Scientists have uncovered how brewer’s yeast developed its unusually tiny centromeres, the DNA regions that guide chromosome separation during cell division. By studying related yeast species, researchers found centromeres that appear to represent evolutionary halfway points. These structures seem to have formed from retrotransposons—mobile “jumping genes” in the genome. The discovery shows how DNA once considered genomic junk can be transformed into essential chromosome machinery.

environment.title: Light-guided evolution creates proteins that can switch, sense, and compute
environment.summary: Researchers have created a method called optovolution that uses light to guide the evolution of proteins with dynamic behaviors. By engineering yeast cells so their survival depended on proteins switching states at the right time, scientists could rapidly select the best-performing variants. The technique produced new light-sensitive proteins that respond to different colors and improved optogenetic systems. It even evolved a protein that behaves like a tiny logic gate, activating genes only when two signals are present.

environment.title: Scientists discover hidden species among Borneo’s “fanged frogs”
environment.summary: DNA is revealing that many animals once thought to be a single species may actually be several hidden ones. But research on Bornean fanged frogs shows the line between species can be blurry—an important challenge when deciding what wildlife needs protection most.

environment.title: Lake Coatepeque
environment.summary: Set amid El Salvador’s modern, active volcanic landscape, tranquil blue waters fill a caldera formed by ancient eruptions. The post Lake Coatepeque appeared first on NASA Science .

environment.title: Scientists stunned to find signs of ancient life in a place no one expected
environment.summary: While exploring ancient seabeds in Morocco, scientists discovered strange wrinkle-like textures in deep-water sediments that shouldn’t have been there. These structures are usually made by sunlight-loving microbial mats in shallow waters. But the rocks formed far below the reach of light, suggesting a different explanation. Evidence points to chemosynthetic microbes—organisms powered by chemical reactions—creating the mats in the dark depths of an ancient ocean.

environment.title: Scientists finally reveal why mint feels cold
environment.summary: Scientists have revealed how the body’s microscopic cold sensor, TRPM8, detects both chilly temperatures and the cooling effect of menthol. The discovery finally shows how the sensation of “cool” works at the molecular level—and could inspire new treatments for pain and eye disorders.

environment.title: Bird droppings helped build one of ancient Peru’s most powerful kingdoms
environment.summary: New research suggests seabird guano helped transform the Chincha Kingdom into one of the most prosperous societies in ancient Peru. Chemical clues in centuries-old maize show farmers fertilized their crops with guano gathered from nearby islands, dramatically boosting yields in the desert landscape. The resulting agricultural surplus fueled trade, population growth, and regional influence.

mix_items.summary: Relația dintre (pre)adolescenți, părinți și școală pare, astăzi, mai fragilă ca oricând. Între dorința copiilor de autonomie, nevoia părinților de control și presiunea unui sistem educațional care încearcă să țină pasul cu schimbările sociale, apar tensiuni, neînțelegeri și, uneori, rupturi greu de reparat. Andreia Bodea, directoarea Colegiului Național „Ion Luca Caragiale” din București, vorbește într-un …

mix_items.title: SPRIJIN MEDIA OFERIT PROGRAMELOR FUNDAȚIEI REGALE ÎN ANUL 2025
mix_items.summary: Programul național Telefonul Vârstnicului – 0800 460 001 Sprijinul media a venit de la Spot TV – Pro TV, TVR, Antena 1, Antena 3 CNN, Antena Stars, Prima TV, Prima News, Pro Cinema, Pro Arena, Acasă, Acasă Gold, Trinitas TV, Speranța TV, Antena 3 Deva,... Post-ul SPRIJIN MEDIA OFERIT PROGRAMELOR FUNDAȚIEI REGALE ÎN ANUL 2025 apare prima dată în Fundația Regală Margareta a României .

mix_items.title: Angajăm Asistent Social pentru Telefonul Vârstnicului la București!
mix_items.summary: Cine suntem noi și de cine avem nevoie? Suntem echipa de la Telefonul Vârstnicului, serviciu social licențiat, singura linie telefonică gratuită și confidențială, accesibilă la nivel național, dedicată persoanelor vârstnice, ce oferă consiliere socială și alinarea singurătății. În fiecare an suntem sunați de peste 5,000... Post-ul Angajăm Asistent Social pentru Telefonul Vârstnicului la București! apare prima dată în Fundația Regală Margareta a României .

mix_items.title: Simona, eleva care descoperă viitorul industriei alimentare pas cu pas
mix_items.summary: Un drum construit prin educație Simona este elevă în clasa a XI-a, la specializarea Tehnician în industria alimentară, la un... Articolul Simona, eleva care descoperă viitorul industriei alimentare pas cu pas apare prima dată în World Vision România .

mix_items.title: Agricultura viitorului: World Vision România sprijină elevii din liceele agricole să devină fermierii erei digitale
mix_items.summary: București, aprilie 2026 – Într-o lume în care cererea de hrană crește accelerat, iar presiunea asupra resurselor naturale este tot... Articolul Agricultura viitorului: World Vision România sprijină elevii din liceele agricole să devină fermierii erei digitale apare prima dată în World Vision România .

mix_items.title: Psiholog
mix_items.summary: Locație: București | Nivel carieră: Experiență de minimum 1 an Candidatul Ideal: Ești tu vocea de care au nevoie? Un copil are nevoie de iubire, siguranță și încredere pentru a... The post Psiholog appeared first on SOS Satele Copiilor România .

mix_items.title: „Școala e drumul vostru”. Învățăturile unei mame cu 10 clase.
mix_items.summary: Într-o casă modestă din județul Vaslui, unde mirosul de lemne arse se amestecă cu zgomotul pașilor mici de copii și... Articolul „Școala e drumul vostru”. Învățăturile unei mame cu 10 clase. apare prima dată în World Vision România .

mix_items.title: Diaspora distress: When geopolitical conflict follows immigrant workers into the office
mix_items.summary: Rostam does not sleep through the night anymore. At 2 a.m., when his phone buzzes, he's awake before the sound finishes. It might be his parents calling from Tehran, on a connection that is unreliable, sporadic and sometimes cut off mid-sentence. He has learned not to miss those calls, because the next one may not come for days.

mix_items.title: De Paște, 900 de vârstnici singuri primesc prezență umană, ascultare și speranță
mix_items.summary: În timp ce pentru mulți români Paștele este sărbătoarea revederii și a meselor în familie, pentru mulți vârstnici singuri aceste zile sunt printre cele mai grele din an. Singurătatea doare, lipsurile și sentimentul de abandon se simt mai apăsat tocmai atunci când toți ceilalți par... Post-ul De Paște, 900 de vârstnici singuri primesc prezență umană, ascultare și speranță apare prima dată în Fundația Regală Margareta a României .

mix_items.title: Sprijinul Raiffeisen Bank schimbă vieți. Povestea lui Victor din programul „Învață să reușești”
mix_items.summary: United Way România lucrează acolo unde nevoia este cea mai mare, acolo unde sprijinul nu este doar util, ci vital. Fără acest ajutor, mulți copii ar rămâne în urmă, riscând să se piardă în dificultățile vieții. De aceea, apreciem sprijinul pe termen lung pe care Raiffeisen Bank îl oferă programului „Învață să reușești”. Printre copiii […] Articolul Sprijinul Raiffeisen Bank schimbă vieți. Povestea lui Victor din programul „Învață să reușești” apare prima dată în United Way Romania .

mix_items.title: Need to see a psychologist? How to find one that's right for you
mix_items.summary: After weeks (or months) of putting it off, you book a longer appointment with your GP to talk about your mental health. You explain that you've been feeling low, anxious and overwhelmed. They suggest seeing a psychologist. But how do you know if this psychologist is the right person for you?

mix_items.title: Bullyingul în adolescență: răni invizibile și cum pot fi vindecate
mix_items.summary: De ce vorbim despre bullying Bullyingul nu mai este de mult o problemă „minoră” sau o etapă inevitabilă a copilăriei.... Articolul Bullyingul în adolescență: răni invizibile și cum pot fi vindecate apare prima dată în World Vision România .

mix_items.title: Webb and Hubble find massive star clusters emerge faster
mix_items.summary: Astronomers using the NASA/ESA/CSA James Webb Space Telescope together with the NASA/ESA Hubble Space Telescope have looked deeply at thousands of young star clusters in four nearby galaxies, studying clusters at different stages of evolution. Their findings show that more massive star clusters emerge more quickly from the clouds they are born in, clearing away gas and filling the galaxy with ultraviolet light. The result gives us a better understanding of star formation in galaxies, as well as how and where planets can form.

mix_items.title: What can singing mice say about human speech?
mix_items.summary: Speech is a crowning achievement of human evolution, the skill that separates us from every other animal. So, it would stand to reason that evolving this capability required some enormous leap in brain complexity. A study published in Nature suggests otherwise.

mix_items.title: Blood test reveals nine tumor cell 'neighborhoods' tied to immunotherapy outcomes
mix_items.summary: A simple blood test can reveal the geographic relationships among healthy cells surrounding a cancerous tumor, researchers at Stanford Medicine and the Mayo Clinic have found. The test is the first noninvasive way to study what's called the tumor microenvironment, which plays a critical role in determining how different patients—even those with similar tumors—fare after diagnosis and treatment.

mix_items.title: How to build cities for wildlife, not just people
mix_items.summary: In central Seoul, South Korea, a motorway once covered a buried urban stream. Today, that same stretch has been uncovered—a process known as daylighting—and this river is home to plants, fish and insects. This flowing water cools the city in summer and attracts tens of thousands of people every day. What used to be concrete now boosts biodiversity, the local economy and community well-being.

mix_items.title: Testosterone suppresses brain tumor growth in males, study suggests
mix_items.summary: In a new study, scientists at Cleveland Clinic discovered that hormones associated with male development may play a key role in limiting the growth of brain tumors in men. The research team found that the loss of androgen hormones, such as testosterone, in a preclinical model of glioblastoma drove tumor growth by inducing local inflammation and triggering the production of stress hormones.

mix_items.title: 1,000 more steps: A simple, powerful prescription for surgical recovery
mix_items.summary: Researchers analyzing data from the National Institutes of Health's All of Us Research Program have found that a patient's step count following surgery is a powerful, objective predictor of recovery, outperforming other metrics such as heart rate variability and self-reported wellness. The study is published in the Journal of the American College of Surgeons.

mix_items.title: Hunters' appreciation of a targeted deer-management program transcends harvest
mix_items.summary: Too many white-tailed deer are damaging forests in the U.S. by eating young plants before they can grow, limiting forest regeneration and damaging biodiversity. To mitigate this challenge, the Pennsylvania Game Commission implemented an initiative called the Deer Management Assistance Program (DMAP) that helps landowners manage deer populations by allowing hunters to harvest more female deer where they are overabundant. While hunters largely value and enjoy hunting in DMAP areas, they have some sentiments that may help inform wildlife management, according to a new study by Penn State researchers.

mix_items.title: Scientists find blood-based biomarkers for inflammatory breast cancer
mix_items.summary: Researchers at The University of Texas MD Anderson Cancer Center and The University of Texas at Austin have identified specific blood-based genomic biomarkers that distinguish inflammatory breast cancer from other subtypes, providing a new and less invasive method for early diagnosis, disease progression monitoring and treatment development for patients with this aggressive disease.

mix_items.title: The lost koala: New fossil species was hiding in plain sight for 100 years
mix_items.summary: In 2024, the Western Australian Museum received a donation. It was a koala skull collected from Moondyne Cave in Margaret River by Lindsay Hatcher, an avid caver. There was something a bit odd about this skull, and we were able to put our finger in it.

mix_items.title: What working‑class boys need to succeed at school: Respect and open conversations
mix_items.summary: Across the UK, working-class boys are navigating an unprecedented convergence of pressures. There are entrenched gaps between working-class boys and their peers in their levels of attainment at every stage of education.

mix_items.title: Landsat 9 captures Russia's restless Shiveluch volcano mid-eruption
mix_items.summary: Near-constant activity continues on the volcano in Russia. Shivelyuch (also called Shiveluch), the most northerly active volcano on the Kamchatka Peninsula, is one of the most active volcanoes in the world. On a near-daily basis, satellites detect new signs of activity within its horseshoe-shaped caldera, including thermal anomalies, hot avalanches and debris flows, and ash deposits that darken the surrounding landscape.

mix_items.title: Concerns raised on gaps in health care for released prisoners
mix_items.summary: People leaving prison in England can experience avoidable gaps in their medication because of fragmented health care systems, poor information sharing, and discharge processes which are sometimes rushed due to release procedures, a new study has revealed.

mix_items.title: NASA’s Roman Poised to Transform Hunt for Elusive Neutron Stars
mix_items.summary: Astronomers have long known that neutron stars, the crushed cores left behind after massive stars explode, should be scattered throughout the Milky Way galaxy. However, most of them are effectively invisible. A new study published in Astronomy and Astrophysics suggests NASA’s upcoming Nancy Grace Roman Space Telescope could spot them anyway. Using detailed simulations of […]

mix_items.title: New NASA Technology Mimics Extreme Cold of the Lunar Night
mix_items.summary: As NASA looks to explore the Moon, Mars, and beyond, researchers must develop materials capable of withstanding the extreme temperatures found in space and on other planets and their moons. In frigid conditions, rubber can shatter like glass, circuit boards may fail, and electrical connections can freeze and fracture.  Gaining a deeper understanding of how […]

mix_items.title: Feeling underqualified can help drive performance or toxic behavior—depending on one psychological factor
mix_items.summary: We've all been there: staring at a job description or a daunting new project and feeling a cold prickle of dread. You have the degree, maybe even the title, but looking at the task ahead, you feel like a total fraud. You're "underqualified"—and it feels like only a matter of time before everyone else figures it out.

mix_items.title: Why surgery still looks like an old boys' club
mix_items.summary: While entry into medicine and surgery has become more diverse, why does that diversity disappear at senior levels? A new study from the University of Surrey argues that the answer lies in how careers are judged day-to-day.

mix_items.title: Lying down to stand stronger: 10 minutes of simple floor exercises a day could improve balance and agility
mix_items.summary: Don't feel like standing up for a workout? No problem, you can still get a good workout that helps improve static standing balance, flexibility, and agility, all while lying on your back with your head facing up.

mix_items.title: Hologram technology where 'light becomes the key' enables hard-to-copy security
mix_items.summary: A new type of hologram technology has been developed that uses the motion of light as a key, revealing information only under specific conditions. This is gaining attention as a novel approach that can simultaneously overcome the limitations of existing optical communication and security technologies.

mix_items.title: Baltimore emergency rooms see spike in asthma-related cases after night heat waves
mix_items.summary: Hospitals in Baltimore see an uptick in asthma-related emergency room cases in the weeks following nighttime heat waves in their neighboring communities, according to new research from Johns Hopkins University.

mix_items.title: At-home blood test and brain testing could screen people for dementia risk
mix_items.summary: A finger prick blood test combined with online brain testing—all done from home—could one day effectively identify people's risk of developing dementia, according to a new study.

mix_items.title: Freshwater mussel protein offers new source of inspiration for medical-grade glues
mix_items.summary: Researchers at the University of Toronto have identified a protein from the quagga mussel that can stick to surfaces underwater, even though it lacks a chemical feature long thought to be essential for this kind of adhesion. The protein, called Dbfp7, is the first freshwater mussel adhesive protein to be functionally characterized.

mix_items.title: This 2-pound dinosaur is rewriting what scientists know about evolution
mix_items.summary: A nearly complete dinosaur skeleton discovered in Patagonia is helping scientists crack the mystery of alvarezsaurs, a bizarre group of bird-like dinosaurs. The fossil of Alnashetri cerropoliciensis reveals that these animals became tiny before developing their later specialized features, such as stubby arms and ant-eating adaptations. Weighing under two pounds, the dinosaur is one of the smallest known from South America.

mix_items.title: Scientists finally solve the mystery of yeast’s tiny centromeres
mix_items.summary: Scientists have uncovered how brewer’s yeast developed its unusually tiny centromeres, the DNA regions that guide chromosome separation during cell division. By studying related yeast species, researchers found centromeres that appear to represent evolutionary halfway points. These structures seem to have formed from retrotransposons—mobile “jumping genes” in the genome. The discovery shows how DNA once considered genomic junk can be transformed into essential chromosome machinery.

mix_items.title: Light-guided evolution creates proteins that can switch, sense, and compute
mix_items.summary: Researchers have created a method called optovolution that uses light to guide the evolution of proteins with dynamic behaviors. By engineering yeast cells so their survival depended on proteins switching states at the right time, scientists could rapidly select the best-performing variants. The technique produced new light-sensitive proteins that respond to different colors and improved optogenetic systems. It even evolved a protein that behaves like a tiny logic gate, activating genes only when two signals are present.

mix_items.title: Scientists discover hidden species among Borneo’s “fanged frogs”
mix_items.summary: DNA is revealing that many animals once thought to be a single species may actually be several hidden ones. But research on Bornean fanged frogs shows the line between species can be blurry—an important challenge when deciding what wildlife needs protection most.

mix_items.title: Lake Coatepeque
mix_items.summary: Set amid El Salvador’s modern, active volcanic landscape, tranquil blue waters fill a caldera formed by ancient eruptions. The post Lake Coatepeque appeared first on NASA Science .

mix_items.title: Scientists stunned to find signs of ancient life in a place no one expected
mix_items.summary: While exploring ancient seabeds in Morocco, scientists discovered strange wrinkle-like textures in deep-water sediments that shouldn’t have been there. These structures are usually made by sunlight-loving microbial mats in shallow waters. But the rocks formed far below the reach of light, suggesting a different explanation. Evidence points to chemosynthetic microbes—organisms powered by chemical reactions—creating the mats in the dark depths of an ancient ocean.

mix_items.title: Scientists finally reveal why mint feels cold
mix_items.summary: Scientists have revealed how the body’s microscopic cold sensor, TRPM8, detects both chilly temperatures and the cooling effect of menthol. The discovery finally shows how the sensation of “cool” works at the molecular level—and could inspire new treatments for pain and eye disorders.

mix_items.title: Bird droppings helped build one of ancient Peru’s most powerful kingdoms
mix_items.summary: New research suggests seabird guano helped transform the Chincha Kingdom into one of the most prosperous societies in ancient Peru. Chemical clues in centuries-old maize show farmers fertilized their crops with guano gathered from nearby islands, dramatically boosting yields in the desert landscape. The resulting agricultural surplus fueled trade, population growth, and regional influence.

joke_ro.text: Ce nivel de motivatie ai azi? - Decorativ.

satire_ro.title: Directorul Poştei Române, despre noua siglă a lui Dinamo: E bestială!
